HPC scheduler systems act as the conductors of this intricate dance, ensuring efficient resource allocation, optimized job execution, and overall system performance. • Open-Source Schedulers: –Slurm: A widely popular, mature, and feature-rich scheduler known for its scalability, fairness policies, and extensive customization options. –Torque/Maui: Another popular option offering good performance and integra- tion with various resource management systems. –PBS Pro: A widely used scheduler with a strong focus on security and user management. –OpenLava: A newer, open-source scheduler designed with cloud-native princi- ples, offering flexibility and scalability for modern HPC environments. • Commercial Schedulers –PlatformLSF:Acomprehensivesuiteforworkloadmanagementandjobschedul- ing, offering advanced features like workload optimization and resource predic- tion. –Altair PBS Professional: A commercially supported version of PBS Pro with additional features like workload visualization and analytics. –Bright Cluster Manager: A highly scalable scheduler with a focus on ease of use and integration with various HPC environments. –Grid Engine (SGE): A mature and widely used scheduler offering good perfor- mance and support for diverse workloads. Slurm, a highly efficient job scheduler, allocates resources (CPUs, GPUs, memory) to different jobs in the cluster, ensuring optimal utilization. 133.2.4 Multi-GPU Systems The heart of a deep learning cluster lies not only in its network of connected nodes, but also in the individual nodes themselves. This is where multi-GPU systems come into play, serving as the multi-engine locomotives that propel your deep learning workloads forward at breakneck speed. Multi-GPU systems offer faster training times by reducing communication overhead over the network across multiple nodes. 14Chapter 4 Installation and Configuration with NVIDIA DeepOps NVIDIA DeepOps provides a robust foundation for your deep learning endeavors, but the true magic lies in optimizing it for your specific needs. Some clusters might offer a web-based interface or dashboard facilitating job submission, monitoring, and management. 5.2 Submitting Deep Learning Training Jobs Running computationally demanding tasks on HPC clusters requires efficient resource management. This is where Slurm comes in, a powerful and open-source workload man- ager and a highly efficient job scheduler used on many HPC systems. Slurm helps users submit jobs, allocate resources like CPU cores and memory, and monitor their progress. Slurm offers a multitude of features beneficial for both users and administrators. Users can track job progress and resource utilization, submit parallel jobs across multiple nodes, and manage dependencies between tasks. Administrators can define different queues for prioritizingjobs, setresourcelimits, andimplementadvancedschedulingalgorithms. By leveraging Slurm’s capabilities, HPC users can maximize their productivity and op- timize resource usage, leading to faster completion times and cost-effective computa- tions. 5.2.1 Useful commands in SLURM To get detailed idea of all SLURM commands, we request you to refer SLURM Doc- umentation. Here, we will briefly discuss about only few commands which are most needed. • sinfo: View information about nodes and partitions (docs) 18Option Description -o, –format=<options> Output format to display -l, –long Show more available information -N, –Node Show information in a node-oriented for- mat -n, –nodes=<hostnames> Filter by host names (comma-separated list) -p, –partition =< partition_list> Filter by partitions (comma-separated list) -t, –states =< state_list> Filter by node states (comma-separated list) -s, –summarize Show summary information Table 5.1: Options for sinfo command • salloc: Obtain a job allocation for interactive use (docs) • sbatch - Submit a batch script for later execution (docs) • srun - Obtain a job allocation and run an application (docs) • scancel - Cancel a job. Syntax - scancel < job_id > • squeue - View information about jobs in scheduling queue (docs) • scontrol - View and modify various aspects of SLURM’s configuration and state. 5.2.2 Running an interactive job Loading the Slurm module gives you access to several commands, one of which is srun. There are several different ways to use this command. Slurm then schedules the job based on its priority, available resources, and queue policies. This ensures efficient uti- lization of the cluster by preventing resource conflicts and optimizing job scheduling. You can submit a job with thesbatch command such as - sbatch job . slurm 5.2.4 Job Submission Scripts for Deep Learning Here’s an example job submission script tailored for a deep learning task, highlighting GPU utilization and memory requirements: #!/ bin / bash # SBATCH -- job - name = dee p_ le ar ni ng_ jo b # SBATCH -- partition = gpu # SBATCH -- nodes =1 # SBATCH -- gres = gpu :1 # SBATCH -- cpus - per - task =8 # SBATCH -- mem =32 G # SBATCH -- time =24:00:00 # Command to run deep learning training script 22python train . py -- dataset / path / to / dataset -- model resnet50 -- batch - size 64 -- epochs 50 -- gpu # Additional commands for post - processing , saving models , etc . -- job - name : Specifies the name of the job . -- partition : Indicates the partition or queue for job execution ( e . g . , 'gpu ' for nodes with GPUs ) . -- nodes : Specifies the number of nodes required for the job . -- gres = gpu :1: Requests one GPU for the job . -- cpus - per - task : Sets the number of CPU cores per task . -- mem : Allocates memory for the job (32 GB in this example ) . -- time : Specifies the maximum time the job is allowed to run (24 hours in this case ) . The script includes loading necessary modules (like CUDA) and activating a Python virtual environment before executing the actual deep learning training script (train.py). X with the CUDA version source activate my_env # Activate Python virtual environment 5.2.5 Considerations • Resource Allocation: Specify appropriate resource requests (e.g., GPUs, memory) matching the requirements of the deep learning workload. • Optimizations: Customize job scripts to optimize GPU usage, memory utilization, and other parameters based on the specific deep learning model and dataset. • Virtual Enviournment: Always run your code in a virtual environment. This will keep your resources isolated from others. • Use Default User: It is suggested to create a folder in the home directory of the default user ubuntu and keep your all resources (data, codes) inside that folder. This will help to utilize the storage resource properly. Job submission scripts tailored for deep learning tasks help specify resource requirements, allowing efficient execution of training tasks on the cluster. 23Chapter 6 Monitoring, Management and Maintenance The journey of deep learning doesn’t stop at building your DeepOps Slurm cluster. Toaddanode you have to just edit the inventory file and add install the DeepOps cluster again. 25Chapter 7 Parallelism & Optimizaion in Deep Learning Deep learning models are becoming increasingly complex, demanding ever-greater com- putational resources to train effectively. This is where parallelism comes in, playing a crucial role in accelerating the training process and pushing the boundaries of what’s possible. But with various types of parallelism available, choosing the right approach can be daunting. Let’s delve into the different options and understand their strengths and limitations. Large deep learning models require significant amount of memory to train. Models require memory to store intermediate activations, weights etc.. In the modern machine learning the various approaches to parallelism are used to: • Fit very large models onto limited hardware. • Significantly speed up training. We will first discuss about various types of parallelism techniques and their pros and cons and then look at how they can be combined to enable an even faster training and to support even bigger models. Various other powerful alternative approaches will be presented throughout this chapter. While the main concepts most likely will apply to any other framework, this chapter is focused on PyTorch-based implementations. 7.1 Parallelism The following is the brief description of the main concepts that will be described later in depth in this document. • DataParallel (DP) - the same setup is replicated multiple times, and each be- ing fed a slice of the data. The processing is done in parallel and all setups are synchronized at the end of each training step. • TensorParallel (TP)- each tensor is split up into multiple chunks, so instead of having the whole tensor reside on a single gpu, each shard of the tensor resides on its designated gpu. During processing each shard gets processed separately and in parallel on different GPUs and the results are synced at the end of the step. This 26is what one may call horizontal parallelism, as the splitting happens on horizontal level. • PipelineParallel (PP)- the model is split up vertically (layer-level) across mul- tiple GPUs, so that only one or several layers of the model are places on a single gpu. Each gpu processes in parallel different stages of the pipeline and working on a small chunk of the batch. To get a better understanding of these parallelism we recommend you to go through these two beautiful blog - • Parallelism in Distributed Deep Learning - Blog by Insu Jang, Ph.D. Candidate in CSE at the University of Michigan • Model Parallelism - Blog by Hugging Face. These parallelism techniques can be combined with each others to accelerate training. Before we discuss about different other training and optimization techniques for training large models we recommend you to go through this survey paper- Colossal-AI: A Unified Deep Learning System For Large-Scale Parallel Training 7.2 DifferentTrainingandOptimizationTechniques In this section we will discuss about different training and optimization techniques for deep learning models. 1. Fully Sharded Data Parallelism (FSDP): FSDP is a type of data-parallel training, but unlike traditional data-parallel, which maintains a per-GPU copy of a model’s parameters, gradients and optimizer states, it shards all of these states across data- parallel workers and can optionally offload the sharded model parameters to CPUs. We recommend you to go through these resources for a detailed understanding on FSDP. • Fully Sharded Data Parallel: faster AI training with fewer GPUs • Introducing PyTorch Fully Sharded Data Parallel (FSDP) API 2. ZeRO: Zero Redundancy Optimizer(ZeRO) is a technique that eliminates memory redundancies by partitioning the optimizer, gradient, and parameters rather than replicating them to utilize the whole available memory. We recommend you to go through these beautiful resources for a better understanding on ZeRO - • ZeRO: Memory Optimizations Toward Training Trillion Parameter Models - Microsoft Paper • ZeRO-Offload: Democratizing Billion-Scale Model Training - Microsoft Paper • ZeroRedundancyOptimizers: AMethodfortrainingMachineLearningModels with Billion Parameters - Blog from Research, Oracle Health & AI • DeepSpeed: Extreme-scale model training for everyone - Blog from Microsoft Research 277.3 Popular Frameworks for Large scale Training There are several popular libraries for large scale model training. Some of these are - • Accelerate - Developed by Hugging face • DeepSpeed - Developed by Microsoft • FairScale - by Facebook AI research. 28Chapter 8 Additional Resources 8.1 Contact Us • WestronglyrecommendyoutojointhisGooglegrouptostayupdateforourupdated version of this document and related discussions - https://groups.google.com/g/tcg- crest-deep-learning/about • For any kind of query, feel free to reach out to Mr. Arindam Majee via ma- jeearindam06072002@gmail.com or visit https://arindammajee.github.io/ to find a preferred method of contacting. 8.2 NVIDIA DeepOps Documentation • Official Documentation: https://github.com/NVIDIA/deepops • Deploying a Scalable GPU-as-a-Service Platform and Building a Deep Learning Project in Under 80 Minutes - NVIDIA OnDemand Video Session 29

llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: A practitioner-oriented guide addressing the problem of how to design, deploy, and operate an on-prem GPU cluster for deep learning when single machines become bottlenecks. Contributions include: (1) an end-to-end blueprint of DeepOps+Slurm-based cluster architecture (compute/GPU, networking, storage) and the rationale for clusters vs single nodes; (2) concrete, step-by-step deployment instructions using NVIDIA DeepOps (Ansible-based) to provision a Slurm controller and compute nodes; (3) operational guidance for access (SSH), job submission (srun/sbatch scripts), monitoring (Slurm commands plus Prometheus/Grafana), maintenance and scaling; and (4) a conceptual overview of distributed training/parallelism options (data, tensor, pipeline parallelism) and memory-optimization techniques (FSDP, ZeRO) and related frameworks (DeepSpeed, Accelerate, FairScale).
llm_extracted_info.methodology: Uses a systems-engineering and DevOps methodology rather than proposing a novel algorithm: (1) infrastructure design patterns for GPU clusters (node roles, star topology networking, shared/distributed storage); (2) automated configuration management via NVIDIA DeepOps, leveraging Ansible inventory/group_vars and playbooks (e.g., playbooks/slurm-cluster.yml, slurm-validation.yml) to install and configure Slurm, monitoring stack, and optional services; (3) standard Slurm workload-manager primitives for interactive and batch execution (srun, salloc, sbatch, squeue, scontrol) with example resource directives for GPU jobs; (4) recommended software stack components for GPU acceleration (CUDA, cuDNN, TensorRT and related CUDA libraries) and guidance for multi-GPU/distributed training concepts (DP/TP/PP, FSDP, ZeRO).
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup: No empirical experiments or benchmarks are reported. Validation is operational/configuration-focused: (1) connectivity/config correctness checks using Ansible ad-hoc commands (e.g., ansible all -m raw -a "hostname"); (2) post-install sanity checks using Slurm introspection (sinfo) and an optional DeepOps-provided Slurm validation playbook (slurm-validation.yml) across nodes; (3) illustrative runtime checks like running nvidia-smi via srun across multiple nodes; (4) monitoring setup verification via Grafana/Prometheus endpoints and node-level tools (top/htop/nvidia-smi). No datasets, ML benchmarks, or quantitative performance evaluation are included.
llm_extracted_info.limitations: (1) Not a research paper with novel methods; primarily a tutorial/guide, so claims about speedups and scalability are not backed by measured results. (2) Lacks reproducible performance benchmarks, cost analysis, energy analysis, or comparative evaluation of network/storage choices. (3) Assumes NVIDIA GPU ecosystem (CUDA-centric) and Linux distributions supported by DeepOps; guidance may not transfer directly to AMD/Intel accelerators or other schedulers. (4) Some recommendations are high-level (e.g., parallelism/optimization techniques) without implementation details, tuning recipes, or failure-mode analysis. (5) Security/operational hardening is lightly covered; e.g., encourages a shared 'ubuntu' user and passwordless SSH, which may be unsuitable for production multi-tenant environments. (6) Networking guidance is conceptual; no concrete topology diagrams, latency/bandwidth requirements by workload, or detailed configuration (RDMA/GPUDirect) procedures.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions: (1) Provide reproducible benchmarking suites for DeepOps+Slurm GPU clusters: scaling efficiency (strong/weak scaling), interconnect comparisons (Ethernet vs InfiniBand), storage backends (Lustre/Ceph/BeeGFS), and multi-GPU node configs. (2) Develop reference architectures and automated profiles for different DL workloads (CV, LLM pretraining, inference) including NCCL/RDMA/GPUDirect tuning and Slurm topology-aware scheduling. (3) Integrate stronger MLOps: container/image management, artifact tracking, experiment management, CI/CD for cluster configs, and reproducible environment modules. (4) Enhance security and multi-tenancy: LDAP/OIDC integration, per-user isolation, quotas/fairshare, secrets management, and network segmentation. (5) Extend automation to hybrid/on-demand elasticity (cloud bursting), Kubernetes-Slurm interoperability, and GPU-as-a-Service portals (e.g., Open OnDemand) with policies. (6) Add operational reliability studies: HA Slurm setups, failure injection, monitoring/alerting playbooks, and automated remediation. (7) Include validated recipes for distributed training stacks (DeepSpeed/FSDP/ZeRO) under Slurm with example configs and throughput/accuracy trade-offs.
